Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Early Modern

Thomas Reid

1710 – 1796

Scottish common sense: perception, belief, and the rejection of the theory of ideas.

Thomas Reid’s philosophy of mind is best read as a realist, theistic, powers-based philosophy of the soul. Its central claim is not merely that we are entitled to believe in external objects or common sense; it is that a mind or soul is an active, enduring subject of original powers, directly conscious of its own operations, directly related to many of its objects, naturally fitted for social and moral life, and capable of free agency. Reid’s mature account is spread chiefly across An Inquiry into the Human Mind on the Principles of Common Sense (1764), Essays on the Intellectual Powers of Man (1785), and Essays on the Active Powers of Man (1788). The secondary literature is right to emphasize perception and epistemology, but Reid’s own project is wider: it includes consciousness, memory, conception, judgment, reasoning, taste, testimony, moral perception, will, liberty, divine cognition, animal mentality, and possible non-human created minds.

The final systematic pair of works, the Intellectual Powers and Active Powers, should be treated together. The critical edition of the Intellectual Powers describes Reid’s positive contribution as a detailed account of the mind’s innate powers, and it notes that the edition is accompanied by manuscript lectures on the nature and immortality of the soul. The same edition’s table of contents places those “Three Lectures on the Nature and Duration of the Soul” after the essay on taste; a Notre Dame review notes that in these lectures Reid uses “soul” and “mind” interchangeably and addresses whether the soul is immaterial, whether it has a place, and whether there is reason to think it perishes at death.

1. Method: a science of mind, not speculative psychology

Reid’s philosophy of mind is deliberately anti-hypothetical. He wants a disciplined “anatomy” or natural history of the powers of the human mind, not a speculative construction from hidden ideas, impressions, brain-vibrations, or assumed mechanisms. The sources of this science are consciousness, careful reflection, common language, and human conduct. He insists that consciousness gives us the existence of present mental operations, but that ordinary consciousness alone is not enough for scientific clarity; careful reflection is needed to distinguish operations, relations, and powers.

This is why Reid’s “common sense” is often misunderstood. It is not an appeal to uneducated prejudice, majority opinion, or anti-intellectual sentiment. In his technical use, common sense is the level of judgment presupposed by ordinary conversation, business, responsibility, and rational life. He explicitly connects “sense” with judgment: good sense is good judgment, and common sense is the judgment common to those with whom we can converse and transact.

The first principles of common sense are not demonstrable conclusions. Their evidence is intuitive rather than inferential. Reid’s point is that reasoning itself depends on principles that cannot be proven by prior reasoning. For example, that one thinks, remembers, reasons, and performs conscious mental operations is a first principle: consciousness is the only evidence we have or can have for the existence of such present operations, and any demand for proof that consciousness is not deceptive already presupposes cognitive trust.

This method yields a philosophy of mind that is at once epistemological, metaphysical, and anthropological. The French reception has often seen this more clearly than the narrower Anglophone focus on anti-skeptical epistemology: a Persée review of Daniel Schulthess’s French monograph reports that Reid considered philosophy an “étude de l’esprit humain,” even a kind of “pneumatologie,” and linked it with an inductive method; a Cairn review similarly characterizes Reid’s project as a rigorous “science de l’homme” or “épistémologie de l’esprit.”

2. What is the mind or soul?

Reid defines the mind by its operations, not by a claimed insight into its hidden essence. The mind is that in a human being which thinks, remembers, reasons, and wills. He says our essence is unknown, as is the essence of body, but nature teaches us to attribute our various thoughts and operations to one internal principle, which is called the mind or soul.

This gives Reid a modest but real substance dualism. “Modest,” because he does not pretend to know the real essence of mind; “real,” because he refuses to reduce the mind to a bundle of perceptions, a collection of ideas, a brain-state, or a modification of matter. He says a mind is a thinking, intelligent, active being, but when asked what the thing is to which these attributes belong, he admits we have only an obscure notion of the subject itself. We know the attributes and operations more clearly than the substance or real essence that has them.

This is central. Reid’s view is not: “I have a clear metaphysical picture of an immaterial substance.” It is rather: “The operations of thinking, remembering, willing, perceiving, judging, and acting require a subject; the subject is not identical with the operations; and although the subject’s essence is obscure, the powers and operations are sufficiently known.” Powers cannot float free of subjects. In the Active Powers, he says power is one thing and exertion another; power may exist unexerted, but power is a quality and cannot exist without a subject to which it belongs.

Reid is also careful not to force all reality into a crude body/mind dichotomy. In the Intellectual Powers, he says every existing thing is either corporeal or incorporeal, but he also warns that it is not evident that every incorporeal thing must be endowed with thought; there may be intermediate natures between body and mind of which we know nothing. This matters because it shows that Reid’s dualism is not a reckless two-box ontology. It is a disciplined refusal to identify thought with matter, combined with a broader humility about created being.

3. Mind as active power

Reid treats mental operations as acts or exertions of powers. He knows that not every mental operation is voluntary, but he resists the picture of the mind as merely passive. Thinking, remembering, judging, perceiving, and willing are operations of a living and active subject. He also argues that common language supports the distinction among the agent, the operation, and the object: in “I see the moon,” “I” denotes the mind or person, “see” denotes the operation, and “moon” denotes the object.

This structure is the backbone of Reid’s philosophy of mind. There is the mind or subject; there are operations; there are powers or faculties from which operations arise; and many operations have objects. Faculties are original natural powers that belong to the constitution of the mind, while habits and acquired abilities modify how these powers are exercised.

A Reidian philosophy of mind is therefore a powers ontology. To understand mind is to identify its powers, their operations, their objects, their evidence, and their proper use. This is why the division between intellectual powers and active powers is not a minor publishing accident. Reid’s own table of contents treats perception, memory, conception, abstraction, judgment, reasoning, and taste as intellectual powers, while the Active Powers deals with will, motives, principles of action, moral liberty, and morals.

4. The rejection of the “way of ideas”

Reid’s famous attack on the “way of ideas” is not only a theory of perception; it is a general philosophy of mental intentionality. Philosophers, he says, have confused the act of thinking with an alleged internal object of thought. Common sense and ordinary language distinguish thought from the object thought about. The object of thought is one thing; the thinking of that object is another.

This is the point behind his criticism of Locke, Berkeley, Hume, and the broader representational tradition. If the immediate objects of the mind are ideas, impressions, images, or internal representatives, then the mind is cut off from external things, past events, other persons, and perhaps even itself. Reid thinks this is the source of skepticism. It is also, in his view, a false description of mental life.

Reid’s alternative is not that the mind has no objects. On the contrary, most operations are essentially object-directed: to perceive is to perceive something; to remember is to remember something; to conceive is to conceive something; to love or resent is to love or resent someone or something. His claim is that the object need not be an internal idea. If a person thinks of an object, that object is already the immediate object of thought in the only useful sense. The word “immediate,” when added to “object of thought,” becomes an expletive.

Conception illustrates the point. Reid denies that conceiving a thing produces a distinct mental image that becomes the object of thought. What is called an “image in the mind” is, properly speaking, the act of conceiving itself, not a second entity produced by the act. He also holds that we can conceive fictional or non-existent objects, which undermines the claim that every act of thought requires a presently existing internal object.

This anti-representationalism also has theological significance. Reid criticizes ancient theories that required eternal models or ideas for divine creation. If God can create without pre-existing matter, and can conceive the plan of creation without an independently existing model, then divine intellect does not require an object-image in the way ancient and modern “idea” theories suppose. Here the mind of God is not a decorative theological example; it is a metaphysical limit case that helps Reid expose the false analogy behind representational theories of mind.

5. Consciousness and reflection

Consciousness, for Reid, is the immediate awareness of present operations of one’s own mind. It is not the same as reflection. Consciousness accompanies mental acts; reflection makes those acts objects of attention and study. Everyone awake is conscious of mental operations, but few people carefully reflect on them. This distinction is crucial because it explains why the data of mind are certain, yet the science of mind is difficult.

Consciousness also reveals more than an isolated mental event. Reid rejects the Humean bundle theory. He mocks the idea that the self is merely a succession of ideas and impressions that somehow remembers, judges, reasons, affirms, denies, eats, drinks, and is merry. Mental operations require a subject; the succession is not the subject that has the succession.

Thus Reid’s philosophy of mind contains a direct critique of reductionist process ontologies. A stream of perceptions is not a person. Thoughts do not think themselves. Memories do not remember themselves. Judgments do not judge themselves. The mind is not inferred as a theoretical posit in the way an unobservable physical particle might be posited; rather, it is embedded in the very grammar and structure of conscious operation.

6. Perception: direct realism with sensory signs

Reid’s account of perception is a direct realism, but not a simplistic one. He recognizes bodily organs, nerves, and brain changes as empirical conditions of human perception. We perceive external objects in our present human constitution only by means of organs God has given us; we do not see without eyes or hear without ears. Yet Reid insists that this dependence is known by experience only and does not show that organs are metaphysically necessary to perception as such.

He traces the physiological chain only so far. External objects affect organs, organs affect nerves, nerves affect the brain, and then, he says, the material part ends and the rest is intellectual. We know that perception in us is connected with bodily impressions by fixed laws of nature, but we discover no necessary connection between the bodily impression and the mental act of perception.

The perceptual act contains at least three elements: a conception or notion of the object, an irresistible belief in its present existence, and the non-inferential character of that belief. Reid says that when perception is clear and steady, the existence of the perceived object cannot be doubted. He does not mean that every perceptual judgment is infallible; he means that distinct perception has the authority of a first principle and is not derived from reasoning about internal ideas.

The key distinction is between sensation and perception. Sensation is a feeling in the mind; perception is directed to an external object or quality. In the rose example, the agreeable odor as felt is a sensation; the quality in the rose is the object perceived. The sensation is not the object of perception, nor an inner picture of it; it functions more like a natural sign by which we are led to perceive the external quality.

This allows Reid to avoid both naïve realism and idealism. He does not collapse all qualities into mind-dependent feelings, but neither does he deny that sensations are mental. Sensation is in the mind; the perceived quality belongs to the object. The connection between the two is part of our constitution, not a discovered necessary metaphysical bridge.

7. Memory and personal identity

Reid’s account of memory is one of his most important contributions to philosophy of mind. Memory is not the perception of a present idea that represents the past. It is immediate knowledge of a past event. The present act of remembering must be distinguished from the thing remembered. A distinct memory gives real knowledge and certainty of what is remembered.

This is why Reid rejects Locke’s memory theory of personal identity. Memory gives evidence of identity; it does not constitute identity. His famous stolen-horse analogy makes the point: resemblance may be evidence that a horse is the same horse, but the horse’s identity does not consist in resemblance. Likewise, memory may be evidence that I am the same person who did something, but my identity does not consist in memory or consciousness.

For Reid, a person is an indivisible, enduring subject. The self is not a thought, action, or feeling; it is the subject that thinks, deliberates, resolves, acts, and suffers. Personal identity implies continued existence of the same indivisible self. If identity consisted in the flow of consciousness, no one would be the same person for two moments, and responsibility would collapse.

This is not a marginal issue. Reid’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from moral responsibility. The soul or mind must be an enduring subject if promises, guilt, desert, prudence, repentance, and accountability are to make sense. His epistemology of memory therefore supports his metaphysics of personhood and his ethics of responsibility.

8. Judgment, belief, and first principles

Judgment is not reducible to comparison of ideas. Reid repeatedly criticizes the view that all judgment consists in perceiving agreement or disagreement among ideas. Consciousness that “I think,” perception that “there is an external object,” memory that “this happened,” and moral perception that “this is right” are not generated by comparing mental images. They are original operations or principles of the mind.

Belief is especially important. Reid opposes Hume’s account of belief as vivacity of perception. Belief is a primitive ingredient in several operations: consciousness involves belief in the present operation; perception involves belief in the present external object; memory involves belief in the past event. This makes Reid a major theorist of non-inferential belief.

The first principles of contingent truths include the existence of things distinctly perceived, the reality of what is distinctly remembered, the existence of life and intelligence in fellow human beings, the reliability of natural signs of mental states, and the authority due to testimony. These are not optional cultural habits; they are part of the constitution of human cognition.

The Spanish-language Philosophica entry usefully emphasizes this constitutional side of Reid: first principles form a kind of mental equipment proper to human constitution, grounding our human way of perceiving, understanding, morally acting, judging, and evaluating. It also notes that Reid did not claim to give an exhaustive final list of first principles.

9. Taste, moral perception, and value cognition

Reid’s inclusion of taste in the Intellectual Powers is philosophically important. He treats taste as a power of the mind by which we discern and relish the beauties of nature and excellence in the fine arts. This is not merely subjective feeling. The sense of beauty, he says, includes an agreeable feeling or emotion accompanied by a judgment of excellence in the object; if the judgment is true, there is real excellence in the object.

Moral perception works similarly, though it belongs more fully to the practical and active side of mind. Moral first principles must be immediately discerned, or moral reasoning has no foundation. A person who does not perceive by the light of his own mind that some conduct is right and some wrong would not be a moral agent. In ordinary cases, Reid says, a person of integrity sees his duty without reasoning, as he sees the road.

This makes Reid’s philosophy of mind value-realist. The mind is not only a mechanism for representing facts or receiving sensations. It is fitted to perceive external things, remember the past, recognize other minds, understand testimony, judge beauty, discern duty, and govern action. A full Reidian philosophy of mind must therefore include aesthetics and ethics, not treat them as later applications.

10. Other minds, testimony, and social operations

Reid is not a philosopher of the isolated mind. He treats sociality as original. Some intellectual operations are solitary, but others require intercourse with another intelligent being: asking, answering, commanding, promising, contracting, testifying, and understanding testimony. Reid explicitly says these social operations are not reducible to simple apprehension, judgment, and reasoning.

The belief in other minds is also a first principle. Children are convinced of life and intelligence in those with whom they interact before they can reason. They ask questions, answer questions, show affection and resentment, and engage socially before they possess reflective arguments for other minds.

Language is therefore not merely a private instrument for arranging ideas. Reid says language is primarily fitted for social operations, even though it also aids solitary thought. Testimony is likewise natural: before children can reason about evidence, they must rely on human testimony and authority, or they would perish for lack of knowledge.

This is one of the most fertile parts of Reid’s philosophy of mind for contemporary reconstruction. A Reidian mind is not first a private Cartesian theater that later infers society. It is from the beginning responsive to signs of thought, passion, authority, trust, address, promise, and obligation. Reid’s philosophy of mind is therefore also a proto-philosophy of communication and social cognition.

11. Active powers, will, and free agency

The Active Powers completes the philosophy of mind by showing that the mind is not only cognitive but practical. Reid accepts the ancient division between understanding and will, with the understanding comprising speculative powers and the will comprising active powers. He says human beings are intended to be active, not merely speculative, and that acting properly is of greater practical importance than thinking acutely.

For Reid, active power is not bare event-causation. The active power we know most clearly belongs to beings with understanding and will. Human power can be exerted only by will, and the criterion for imputability is whether something was done by one’s conscious will and intention. What is done without knowledge, will, or intention is not truly imputable to the agent.

Moral liberty is the power a moral agent has over the determinations of his own will. If, in an action, the agent had power to will what he did or not to will it, he is free; if every voluntary action is the necessary consequence of something involuntary in the mind or external circumstances, he lacks moral liberty. This liberty requires understanding, will, and practical judgment.

Motives influence action, but Reid rejects the deterministic claim that motives are the sole causes of action. He argues that it can never be proven that a motive on one side must determine action, and he treats the slogan “the strongest motive always prevails” as often empty unless “strongest” is defined independently of mere success in prevailing.

This yields an agent-causal philosophy of mind. A person is not merely a location where motives battle until the strongest wins. A person is an active subject with powers, capable of acting under reasons, resisting motives, obeying or disobeying duty, and being praised or blamed. The Stanford Encyclopedia accordingly identifies Reid as defending an agent-causal theory of free will.

12. Brute animals and degrees of mind

Reid does not deny mentality to animals. In the Intellectual Powers, he says brute animals seem to have an inferior thinking principle. This is part of his wider taxonomy of minds known by reason: one’s own mind, the minds of other human beings, the inferior thinking principle in animals, and the Supreme Mind.

Yet Reid distinguishes animal mentality from full moral agency. In the Active Powers, he says brutes are governed by instinct, appetite, passion, and discipline, but they do not show conception of law, moral obligation, or intentional obedience in the way moral government requires. He therefore allows animal mentality without granting animals the full structure of rational moral liberty.

The result is a graded philosophy of mind. Mind is not all-or-nothing in the sense that only adult humans have any mental principle. But moral personality, rational liberty, and accountability require powers that brutes appear not to possess, or at least not in the relevant degree.

13. The mind of God

The mind of God is not a theological distraction in Reid. It is a central boundary concept in his philosophy of mind. Reid explicitly includes the Supreme Mind among the minds of which reason can give certain knowledge. Everything around us, he says, convinces us of an eternal, infinite, wise, powerful, and good Mind, the Maker and Governor of all things.

Divine cognition also shapes Reid’s analysis of perception. Human beings now perceive through organs, but Reid denies that organs are necessary to perception in its own nature. He says the Supreme Being perceives everything more perfectly than we do and without bodily organs. He also says we have reason to believe that after we put off our bodies, our perceptive powers will be improved rather than destroyed or impaired.

This matters philosophically. It means Reid’s account of human perception is not a theory of all possible perception. Human sense-organs are contingent limitations of our present state, not the essence of perceptive mind. God’s organless perception reveals that perception, as such, belongs to mind or intelligence, not to the bodily organ. The organ is an instrument in our constitution, not the subject that perceives.

Divine prescience has a similar role. Reid admits that we cannot explain God’s foreknowledge of the actions of free agents, but he argues that we also cannot explain memory of the past actions of free agents. Memory shows that an intelligent being can have certain knowledge of free actions without tracing them from necessary conditions. Because we possess memory but not prescience, we find memory easy to admit and divine prescience hard to conceive; that difficulty reveals the limits of our constitution, not an impossibility in God.

God also enters Reid’s philosophy of agency. Divine government is moral, not merely mechanical, because God has given some creatures, especially humans, reason and active power. Moral government presupposes liberty in those governed; accountability cannot extend beyond liberty. At the same time, created active power remains a gift of God and depends on God for its existence, degree, and continuance.

Thus the mind of God functions in at least four ways: as the Supreme Mind known through the order of creation; as the paradigm of perception without bodily organs; as the possessor of cognitive powers beyond our constitution, such as prescience; and as the free governor whose moral government requires creaturely freedom.

14. Angels and other created minds

Reid’s references to angels and other created intelligences must be read with both seriousness and caution. He warns against a common error: because human beings first know material things through the senses, they are tempted to form their notions of mind by bodily analogy. This leads people to conceive mind as subtle matter and to attribute human figure and organs not only to angels but even to God. Reid rejects this anthropomorphic analogizing.

That warning does not make angels irrelevant. It means that any philosophical use of angelic minds must avoid projecting human bodily form, human sensory organs, or human cognitive limits onto them. In the chapter on organs of sense, Reid says we have reason to believe there are other created beings endowed with powers of perception more perfect and extensive than ours, without such organs as we find necessary. This is very naturally read as including angelic or spiritual created intelligences, though Reid does not build a detailed angelology from it.

The philosophical significance is substantial. Other created minds show that the human mind is not the measure of all mind. Human cognition is embodied, limited, sensory, and developmental; but created cognition as such may exist in higher forms, with different relations to space, organs, and objects. A complete Reidian philosophy of mind therefore includes a modal hierarchy: human minds, animal minds, possible higher created minds, and the Supreme Mind.

The proper Reidian posture toward angels is neither dismissal nor speculative excess. They are not mere “theological distractions,” because they help mark the limits of human embodiment and the contingency of our sensory constitution. But Reid’s own anti-analogical method forbids treating them as human minds with invisible bodies or refined organs. They are important precisely because they unsettle anthropomorphic philosophy of mind.

15. The soul’s immateriality, place, and duration

Reid’s published philosophy presupposes and frequently implies an immaterialist account of mind, but his most direct discussions of the soul as such appear in manuscript material appended to the modern critical edition of the Intellectual Powers. A Notre Dame review reports that in these lectures Reid asks whether the soul or mind is immaterial, whether it has a place, and whether there is reason to think it perishes at death; his answers are, respectively, yes, we cannot be sure, and no. The same review notes that these materials make visible Reid’s positive arguments for immateriality more clearly than the published works do.

A separate review in The Review of Metaphysics, indexed by PhilPapers, states that the appended lectures include Reid’s “naturalistic argument for the immortality of the soul,” connected with the indestructibility of matter. Because the lecture text is manuscript material and not as fully developed as the published essays, it should be used carefully. But it confirms that Reid’s philosophy of mind is also, for him, a philosophy of the soul’s nature and duration.

Even in the published Intellectual Powers, Reid clearly indicates that bodily organs are not necessary to perception as such and that we have reason to believe perceptive powers may be improved after we “put off” our bodies. That is a published, philosophical statement of postmortem cognitive possibility, not merely devotional language.

16. Non-English reception and interpretive gains

A cross-language survey reinforces that Reid should not be reduced to a narrow anti-Humean epistemologist. French sources are especially helpful in emphasizing Reid as a philosopher of “pneumatology,” human nature, and the science of mind. A 2025 French review by Roger Pouivet describes Reid’s common-sense philosophy as identifying natural and irresistible principles and beliefs that condition rational thought, and notes that Reid was widely read in France in the nineteenth century and is now being rediscovered.

Italian sources emphasize the same breadth. Treccani presents Reid and the Scottish school as a reaction to Berkeleyan immaterialism and Humean skepticism, but it stresses that they investigated belief and perception and defended spontaneous natural beliefs shared across peoples, times, and languages. The same article highlights sensation and memory as simple, original operations of mind and notes Reid’s later French reception through Victor Cousin, who saw Scottish philosophy as supporting a sound metaphysics and natural theology.

The Italian journal Discipline Filosofiche devoted a 1996 issue to “Thomas Reid: scienza, filosofia e senso comune,” with articles on Hume and Reid’s science of mind, intentionality and realism in Reid’s philosophy of perception, the concept of the external object, persons, and the nature of the self. That table of contents is significant because it shows an Italian reception attentive to precisely the topics needed for a full philosophy of mind: intentionality, realism, personhood, selfhood, and the science of mind.

Spanish-language reception also helps avoid a shallow reading of common sense. The Philosophica article frames common sense as a set of first principles belonging to human mental constitution, grounding perception, understanding, moral action, and evaluation. It also stresses that Reid’s lists of first principles are plausible candidates rather than an exhaustive deduction of all principles.

These non-English sources do not overturn the Anglophone picture, but they correct its narrowness. They encourage reading Reid as a philosopher of mind, anthropology, soul, value, and natural theology, not merely as a technician of perceptual epistemology.

Extracted Reidian Philosophy of Mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Reid can be stated in the following theses.

First, mind or soul is an active subject of powers. It is not identical with its operations, not a bundle of perceptions, not an idea-container, and not a bodily organ. We know it through its operations: thinking, perceiving, remembering, judging, willing, and acting.

Second, the essence of mind is obscure, but its powers and operations are knowable. Reid’s philosophy is realist but epistemically modest. He does not pretend to know the soul’s hidden essence; he insists that the reality of the subject is required by the operations we know.

Third, mental powers are original parts of our constitution. Perception, memory, consciousness, judgment, testimony-trust, moral discernment, taste, and active power are not derived from a single mechanism of ideas. They are distinct powers with distinct evidences and objects.

Fourth, mental operations are object-directed. Reid’s grammar of mind is triadic: subject, operation, object. This is the foundation of his anti-representationalism.

Fifth, ideas are not the immediate objects of thought. To think of the moon, a friend, a past event, a fictional island, a duty, or God is not to inspect an internal object that represents them. The object of thought is what one thinks about.

Sixth, perception is direct but mediated in our constitution by sensations and organs. Sensations are mental feelings and natural signs; they are not the perceived objects. The perceived object is external.

Seventh, belief is primitive. Consciousness, perception, memory, and moral judgment include forms of non-inferential belief. These are not irrational; they are rationally basic.

Eighth, consciousness gives immediate evidence of present operations, while reflection gives scientific clarity about them. Philosophy of mind requires trained reflection on what consciousness already gives.

Ninth, memory is direct knowledge of the past, not awareness of a present image. Memory also gives evidence of personal identity, though it does not constitute identity.

Tenth, the person is an indivisible enduring self. Personal identity is primitive and cannot be reduced to psychological continuity, memory, or bodily continuity.

Eleventh, the mind is originally social. Belief in other minds, understanding of natural signs, reliance on testimony, and operations such as promising, commanding, questioning, and contracting are fundamental to human mentality.

Twelfth, the mind is value-responsive. Beauty and duty are not merely feelings projected onto objects. Taste and moral judgment include affective response, but also judgment of real excellence or obligation.

Thirteenth, the mind is active and morally free. Human beings possess active power, exercised by will, guided by reason, and accountable under moral law. Motives influence but do not mechanically necessitate free action.

Fourteenth, embodiment is a condition of human mental life in our present state, not the essence of mind. Sense organs are necessary for us now, by divine constitution, but perception without organs is possible for God and for higher created beings.

Fifteenth, philosophy of mind must include comparative mind: animal minds, human minds, higher created minds, and the Supreme Mind. Reid’s philosophy is not merely human psychology; it is a layered account of mental powers across kinds and degrees of being.

Sixteenth, the mind of God is philosophically central. God is the Supreme Mind, perfect perceiver, free governor, and possessor of cognitive powers beyond human constitution. Divine cognition helps reveal the contingency of human organs, images, and temporal limits.

Seventeenth, angelic or higher created minds are philosophically significant but must be treated without anthropomorphism. They mark the possibility of finite, non-human, non-organic cognition, while Reid’s method forbids detailed speculation beyond evidence.

Critical tensions and how Reid manages them

The first tension is between dualism and modesty. Reid is clearly anti-materialist and treats mind as distinct from body, but he also says the real essence of mind is obscure. This is not inconsistency; it is the core of his method. We can know that thought, will, and perception belong to a subject without knowing the subject’s intrinsic essence.

The second tension is between direct realism and the role of sensation. Reid resolves it by distinguishing the sign from the thing signified. Sensation is in the mind and often occasions perception, but the object perceived is not the sensation. This is a direct realism of object, not a denial of sensory mediation.

The third tension is between primitive belief and philosophical rigor. Reid does not argue that first principles are arbitrary. He argues that they are unavoidable, practically indispensable, and presupposed by reasoning itself. They cannot be proven because proof already depends on them.

The fourth tension is between human embodiment and postmortem or angelic cognition. Reid’s solution is to separate empirical necessity for us from metaphysical necessity in itself. In our current constitution, organs are necessary for perception; in God, and perhaps in higher created beings, perception need not involve organs.

The fifth tension is between divine prescience and human freedom. Reid admits that we cannot fully explain divine foreknowledge of free actions, but he denies that this justifies determinism. We also cannot explain memory’s relation to past free actions, yet memory is undeniable. The difficulty reveals the limits of our faculties, not the impossibility of divine knowledge or human liberty.

The sixth tension is between theology and philosophy. Reid does not treat God, the soul, and higher created minds as detachable ornaments. They constrain his philosophy of mind: they block materialism, prevent anthropomorphic psychology, expand the modal range of cognition, and ground the moral government within which active power matters.

In one sentence: Reid’s philosophy of mind is a theistic, direct-realist, anti-representational, agent-causal, social, and value-realist account of the soul as an active enduring subject of original powers, whose human embodiment is real but not essential to mind as such, and whose proper interpretation must include God and higher created minds as philosophically serious limit cases.

PreviousGottfried Wilhelm Leibniz NextDavid Hume

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
